sections.title: Time Value of Money - Core Concepts
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: The time value of money (TVM) is the MOST IMPORTANT concept in finance. It means: A dollar TODAY is worth MORE than a dollar TOMORROW. Why? Because you can invest today's dollar and earn interest on it.

WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE EXAM: Quantitative Methods represents 8-12% of the CFA Level I exam (approximately 14-22 questions out of 180). TVM is the FOUNDATION for everything else - bond valuation, stock valuation, capital budgeting, etc. You MUST master this.

THE BASIC IDEA: If I offer you $1,000 TODAY or $1,000 ONE YEAR FROM NOW, which should you choose? ANSWER: Take it TODAY! Why? Because you can invest it and earn interest. If you can earn 5% interest, that $1,000 today becomes $1,050 in one year. So $1,000 today is WORTH MORE than $1,000 next year.

KEY TERMS:

  • Present Value (PV): What money is worth TODAY. EXAMPLE: If you need $1,050 in one year and can earn 5% interest, you need to invest $1,000 TODAY. So PV = $1,000.
  • Future Value (FV): What money will be worth in the FUTURE. EXAMPLE: If you invest $1,000 today at 5% interest, in one year you'll have $1,050. So FV = $1,050.
  • Interest Rate (r or I/Y): The rate of return you can earn. Also called 'discount rate' or 'required rate of return.' EXAMPLE: 5% per year.
  • Number of Periods (N or n): How many time periods (years, months, etc.). EXAMPLE: 5 years = N=5.

THE BASIC TVM FORMULA:

FV = PV × (1 + r)^N

Where:

  • FV = Future Value
  • PV = Present Value
  • r = Interest rate per period (as a decimal, so 5% = 0.05)
  • N = Number of periods

EXAMPLE: You invest $1,000 at 8% annual interest for 5 years. What will you have?

  • PV = $1,000
  • r = 8% = 0.08
  • N = 5 years
  • FV = $1,000 × (1 + 0.08)^5
  • FV = $1,000 × 1.4693
  • FV = $1,469.33

After 5 years, you'll have $1,469.33.

COMPOUNDING FREQUENCY MATTERS:

The more frequently interest is compounded, the HIGHER your future value will be.

  • Annual compounding: Interest paid once per year
  • Semi-annual compounding: Interest paid twice per year
  • Quarterly compounding: Interest paid 4 times per year
  • Monthly compounding: Interest paid 12 times per year
  • Daily compounding: Interest paid 365 times per year
  • Continuous compounding: Interest paid constantly (maximum possible)

ADJUSTING FOR COMPOUNDING FREQUENCY:

If interest is compounded more than once per year, you must adjust:

  • Divide the interest rate by the number of compounding periods per year (m)
  • Multiply the number of years by m

FORMULA: FV = PV × (1 + r/m)^(N×m)

EXAMPLE: $1,000 invested at 12% annual rate for 2 years, compounded SEMI-ANNUALLY:

  • r = 12% annual, but 6% per semi-annual period (12% ÷ 2)
  • N = 2 years, but 4 semi-annual periods (2 × 2)
  • FV = $1,000 × (1 + 0.06)^4
  • FV = $1,000 × 1.2625
  • FV = $1,262.48

EFFECTIVE ANNUAL RATE (EAR):

The EAR tells you the TRUE annual return when compounding occurs more than once per year.

EAR = (1 + periodic_rate)^m - 1

Where m = number of compounding periods per year

EXAMPLE: 12% stated rate, compounded monthly:

  • Periodic rate = 12% ÷ 12 = 1% per month
  • EAR = (1 + 0.01)^12 - 1
  • EAR = 1.1268 - 1 = 0.1268 = 12.68%

Even though the stated rate is 12%, you actually earn 12.68% due to monthly compounding!

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'MORE = MORE' - More frequent compounding = MORE future value = MORE EAR. Continuous compounding gives the MAXIMUM possible EAR.
keyPoints: Quant = 8-12% of exam (14-22 questions) - THIRD highest weighted topic
Time Value of Money is the FOUNDATION for all valuation
A dollar TODAY is worth MORE than a dollar TOMORROW
FV = PV × (1 + r)^N - MUST KNOW THIS FORMULA
More frequent compounding = HIGHER future value
EAR = (1 + periodic_rate)^m - 1
Continuous compounding gives the MAXIMUM EAR
For semi-annual: divide rate by 2, multiply periods by 2
For monthly: divide rate by 12, multiply periods by 12
Memory aid: 'MORE = MORE' - More compounding = More FV

variables.m: Number of compounding periods per year

practiceQuestions.question: If you invest $5,000 today at 6% annual interest compounded annually, what will be the value after 10 years?
practiceQuestions.explanation: FV = PV × (1 + r)^N = $5,000 × (1 + 0.06)^10 = $5,000 × 1.7908 = $8,954.24. EXAM TIP: Make sure your calculator is set to the correct compounding frequency. For annual compounding, I/Y = 6, N = 10, PV = -5000, CPT FV.

practiceQuestions.question: Which of the following compounding frequencies produces the HIGHEST effective annual rate for a 10% stated annual rate?
practiceQuestions.explanation: Continuous compounding produces the MAXIMUM possible EAR. For 10% stated rate: Annual EAR = 10.00%, Monthly EAR = 10.47%, Continuous EAR = e^0.10 - 1 = 10.517%. EXAM TIP: Remember 'MORE = MORE' - more frequent compounding = higher EAR. Continuous is the maximum.

practiceQuestions.question: The effective annual rate (EAR) for a 12% stated annual rate compounded quarterly is closest to:
practiceQuestions.explanation: EAR = (1 + periodic_rate)^m - 1 = (1 + 0.12/4)^4 - 1 = (1.03)^4 - 1 = 1.1255 - 1 = 12.55%. EXAM TIP: For quarterly compounding, divide the rate by 4 and raise to the 4th power.

sections.title: Annuities and Perpetuities
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: An annuity is a series of EQUAL payments made at REGULAR intervals. A perpetuity is an annuity that goes on FOREVER (infinite payments).

TYPES OF ANNUITIES:

  1. Ordinary Annuity: Payments occur at the END of each period. EXAMPLE: Most bonds pay interest at the end of each period. Mortgage payments are typically ordinary annuities.
  2. Annuity Due: Payments occur at the BEGINNING of each period. EXAMPLE: Rent payments, insurance premiums, lease payments - you pay at the start of the month.

KEY DIFFERENCE: Annuity Due payments happen ONE PERIOD EARLIER, so they have MORE time to earn interest. Therefore, for the same payment amount and interest rate, an Annuity Due has a HIGHER present value and future value than an Ordinary Annuity.

PRESENT VALUE OF ORDINARY ANNUITY:

PV = PMT × [1 - (1 + r)^-N] / r

Where:

  • PV = Present value of the annuity
  • PMT = Payment amount per period
  • r = Interest rate per period
  • N = Number of payments

EXAMPLE: You win a lottery that pays $10,000 per year for 20 years (ordinary annuity). If you can earn 5% on your investments, what is the present value of your winnings?

  • PMT = $10,000
  • r = 5% = 0.05
  • N = 20 years
  • PV = $10,000 × [1 - (1.05)^-20] / 0.05
  • PV = $10,000 × [1 - 0.3769] / 0.05
  • PV = $10,000 × 12.4622
  • PV = $124,622

Even though you'll receive $200,000 total ($10,000 × 20 years), the present value is only $124,622 because of the time value of money.

FUTURE VALUE OF ORDINARY ANNUITY:

FV = PMT × [(1 + r)^N - 1] / r

EXAMPLE: You save $5,000 per year for 30 years in a retirement account earning 7% annually. How much will you have?

  • PMT = $5,000
  • r = 7% = 0.07
  • N = 30 years
  • FV = $5,000 × [(1.07)^30 - 1] / 0.07
  • FV = $5,000 × [7.6123 - 1] / 0.07
  • FV = $5,000 × 94.461
  • FV = $472,305

You only contributed $150,000 ($5,000 × 30), but earned $322,305 in interest!

PERPETUITY (Infinite Annuity):

A perpetuity pays the SAME amount FOREVER. While this sounds unrealistic, it's used to value preferred stock and some bonds (consols).

PV of Perpetuity = PMT / r

EXAMPLE: A preferred stock pays $5 per year forever. If your required return is 8%, what is the stock worth?

  • PMT = $5
  • r = 8% = 0.08
  • PV = $5 / 0.08 = $62.50

The stock is worth $62.50.

ANNUITY DUE ADJUSTMENT:

To convert an Ordinary Annuity value to Annuity Due, MULTIPLY by (1 + r):

  • PV of Annuity Due = PV of Ordinary Annuity × (1 + r)
  • FV of Annuity Due = FV of Ordinary Annuity × (1 + r)

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'BEGIN = BIGGER' - Annuity Due (BEGINning of period) has BIGGER PV and FV than Ordinary Annuity.
keyPoints: Ordinary Annuity: payments at END of each period
Annuity Due: payments at BEGINNING of each period
Annuity Due has HIGHER PV and FV than Ordinary Annuity
PV of Ordinary Annuity = PMT × [1 - (1+r)^-N] / r
FV of Ordinary Annuity = PMT × [(1+r)^N - 1] / r
PV of Perpetuity = PMT / r (simple formula!)
Perpetuity pays FOREVER (infinite payments)
To convert Ordinary to Due: multiply by (1 + r)
Most bonds and mortgages are ordinary annuities
Rent and insurance premiums are annuities due
Memory aid: 'BEGIN = BIGGER' - Annuity Due is bigger

formulas.expression: PV of Ordinary Annuity = PMT × [1 - (1+r)^-N] / r
FV of Ordinary Annuity = PMT × [(1+r)^N - 1] / r

practiceQuestions.question: A preferred stock pays a $6 annual dividend forever. If the required rate of return is 7%, what is the value of the stock?
practiceQuestions.explanation: PV of Perpetuity = PMT / r = $6 / 0.07 = $85.71. EXAM TIP: Preferred stock valuation is a classic perpetuity problem. Remember the simple formula: PV = PMT / r.

practiceQuestions.question: For the same payment amount, interest rate, and number of periods, which has a HIGHER present value?
options: They have the same present value
practiceQuestions.explanation: Annuity Due has a higher present value because payments occur at the BEGINNING of each period, giving each payment one extra period to earn interest. To convert: PV of Annuity Due = PV of Ordinary Annuity × (1 + r). EXAM TIP: Remember 'BEGIN = BIGGER' - payments at the beginning are worth more.

sections.title: Statistical Measures - Central Tendency
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Statistical measures help us summarize and describe data. 'Central tendency' tells us where the 'center' or 'average' of the data is.

THREE MEASURES OF CENTRAL TENDENCY:

  1. Arithmetic Mean (Average): The sum of all values divided by the number of values. FORMULA: Mean = (Sum of all values) / (Number of values). EXAMPLE: Returns of 5%, 10%, 15% → Mean = (5 + 10 + 15) / 3 = 10%. MOST COMMONLY USED measure of central tendency.
  2. Median: The MIDDLE value when data is arranged from smallest to largest. EXAMPLE: Returns of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25% → Median = 15% (the middle value). If there's an even number of observations, take the average of the two middle values. EXAMPLE: 5%, 10%, 15%, 20% → Median = (10% + 15%) / 2 = 12.5%. USEFUL WHEN: Data has OUTLIERS (extreme values). The median is not affected by outliers.
  3. Mode: The value that appears MOST FREQUENTLY. EXAMPLE: Returns of 5%, 10%, 10%, 15%, 20% → Mode = 10% (appears twice). A dataset can have NO mode (all values appear once) or MULTIPLE modes (two or more values appear equally often).

WHEN TO USE EACH:

  • Mean: Use for most situations. BEST for symmetric data without outliers.
  • Median: Use when data has OUTLIERS. EXAMPLE: In a room of 10 people, if 9 earn $50,000 and 1 earns $10 million, the mean income is skewed way up. The median ($50,000) better represents the 'typical' person.
  • Mode: Use for categorical data. EXAMPLE: What's the most common eye color? The mode tells you.

GEOMETRIC MEAN:

The geometric mean is used for COMPOUND GROWTH RATES. It's ALWAYS less than or equal to the arithmetic mean.

Geometric Mean = (X₁ × X₂ × ... × Xₙ)^(1/n)

WHEN TO USE GEOMETRIC MEAN:

  • Calculating average investment returns over multiple periods
  • When values are multiplied together (compounding)
  • When you want the 'compound annual growth rate' (CAGR)

EXAMPLE: An investment earns +20% in Year 1, -10% in Year 2, and +15% in Year 3. What's the average annual return?

WRONG (Arithmetic Mean): (20% + (-10%) + 15%) / 3 = 8.33%

RIGHT (Geometric Mean): [(1.20) × (0.90) × (1.15)]^(1/3) - 1 = 7.70%

The geometric mean (7.70%) is the TRUE average compound return.

HARMONIC MEAN:

Used for averaging MULTIPLES like P/E ratios. It's ALWAYS less than or equal to the geometric mean.

Harmonic Mean = n / (1/X₁ + 1/X₂ + ... + 1/Xₙ)

RELATIONSHIP: For positive numbers: Arithmetic Mean ≥ Geometric Mean ≥ Harmonic Mean

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'AGH' - Arithmetic, Geometric, Harmonic. They go from LARGEST to SMALLEST. AGH! The monster gets smaller!
keyPoints: Arithmetic Mean = Sum of values / Number of values (most common)
Median = Middle value (best when data has outliers)
Mode = Most frequently occurring value
Geometric Mean is used for COMPOUND GROWTH RATES
Geometric Mean is ALWAYS ≤ Arithmetic Mean
Harmonic Mean is used for averaging MULTIPLES (P/E ratios)
Relationship: Arithmetic ≥ Geometric ≥ Harmonic
For investment returns over time, use Geometric Mean
Median is NOT affected by outliers
Memory aid: 'AGH' - Arithmetic, Geometric, Harmonic (largest to smallest)

practiceQuestions.question: An investment has the following annual returns: Year 1: +20%, Year 2: -10%, Year 3: +15%. The geometric mean return is closest to:
practiceQuestions.explanation: Geometric Mean = [(1.20) × (0.90) × (1.15)]^(1/3) - 1 = (1.242)^(1/3) - 1 = 1.0770 - 1 = 7.70%. The arithmetic mean would be (20% + (-10%) + 15%) / 3 = 8.33%, but this OVERSTATES the true compound return. EXAM TIP: For multi-period investment returns, ALWAYS use geometric mean, not arithmetic mean.

practiceQuestions.question: Which measure of central tendency is MOST APPROPRIATE when the dataset contains extreme outliers?
practiceQuestions.explanation: The median is most appropriate when there are outliers because it is NOT affected by extreme values. The arithmetic mean would be pulled toward the outlier. EXAMPLE: Incomes of $50K, $50K, $50K, $50K, $10 million → Mean = $2 million (misleading), Median = $50K (accurate). EXAM TIP: When you see 'outliers' or 'skewed distribution' in a question, think MEDIAN.

sections.title: Statistical Measures - Dispersion
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Dispersion tells us how 'spread out' the data is. Two datasets can have the SAME mean but VERY different dispersion. In investing, dispersion = RISK.

KEY MEASURES OF DISPERSION:

  1. Range: The difference between the largest and smallest values. FORMULA: Range = Maximum value - Minimum value. EXAMPLE: Returns of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25% → Range = 25% - 5% = 20%. SIMPLE but LIMITED - only uses two values, ignores everything else.
  2. Variance: The average of the SQUARED deviations from the mean. Measures how far each value is from the mean, on average. FORMULA (Population): σ² = Σ(X_i - μ)² / N. FORMULA (Sample): s² = Σ(X_i - X̄)² / (n - 1). KEY POINT: Sample variance divides by (n-1), not n. This is called 'Bessel's correction' and corrects for bias.
  3. Standard Deviation: The SQUARE ROOT of variance. This is the MOST IMPORTANT measure of risk in finance. FORMULA: σ = √Variance. WHY USE IT: Standard deviation is in the SAME UNITS as the original data. EXAMPLE: If returns have variance of 0.04, standard deviation = √0.04 = 0.20 = 20%. Much easier to interpret!

STEP-BY-STEP EXAMPLE: Calculate variance and standard deviation for returns: 5%, 10%, 15%

  1. Find the mean: (5 + 10 + 15) / 3 = 10%
  2. Find deviations from mean: (5-10) = -5%, (10-10) = 0%, (15-10) = +5%
  3. Square the deviations: (-5)² = 25, (0)² = 0, (+5)² = 25
  4. Sum of squared deviations: 25 + 0 + 25 = 50
  5. Variance (population): 50 / 3 = 16.67
  6. Variance (sample): 50 / (3-1) = 25
  7. Standard deviation (sample): √25 = 5%

INTERPRETING STANDARD DEVIATION:

  • Low standard deviation = Returns are CLUSTERED near the mean (LOW RISK)
  • High standard deviation = Returns are SPREAD OUT (HIGH RISK)
  • EXAMPLE: Stock A has σ = 10%, Stock B has σ = 30%. Stock B is RISKIER.

COEFFICIENT OF VARIATION (CV):

The CV measures risk PER UNIT OF RETURN. It allows you to compare risk-adjusted performance across investments with different means.

CV = Standard Deviation / Mean

INTERPRETATION: LOWER CV is BETTER (less risk per unit of return).

EXAMPLE: Compare two investments:

  • Investment A: Mean return = 10%, σ = 15% → CV = 15%/10% = 1.5
  • Investment B: Mean return = 15%, σ = 20% → CV = 20%/15% = 1.33

Investment B has a HIGHER absolute risk (20% vs 15%), but a LOWER CV (1.33 vs 1.5). So Investment B has BETTER risk-adjusted performance!

SEMI-VARIANCE AND SEMI-DEVIATION:

These measure ONLY DOWNSIDE risk (returns below the mean or below a target).

  • Some investors care more about LOSSES than gains
  • Semi-variance only considers returns BELOW the mean
  • Useful for asymmetric return distributions

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'VRSC' - Variance, Range, Standard deviation, Coefficient of variation. These are the four dispersion measures, in order of importance for the exam.
keyPoints: Range = Maximum - Minimum (simplest measure)
Variance = Average of SQUARED deviations from mean
Standard Deviation = Square root of Variance (MOST IMPORTANT)
Sample variance divides by (n-1), not n (Bessel's correction)
Standard deviation measures RISK in finance
Coefficient of Variation = Standard Deviation / Mean
LOWER CV is BETTER (less risk per unit of return)
CV allows comparison across investments with different means
Semi-variance measures only DOWNSIDE risk
Memory aid: 'VRSC' - Variance, Range, Standard deviation, CV

practiceQuestions.question: Investment A has a mean return of 12% and standard deviation of 18%. Investment B has a mean return of 8% and standard deviation of 10%. Which investment has a LOWER coefficient of variation?
options: They have the same coefficient of variation
practiceQuestions.explanation: CV of A = 18%/12% = 1.5. CV of B = 10%/8% = 1.25. Investment B has a lower CV, meaning LESS risk per unit of return. Even though Investment A has a higher absolute return, Investment B has better risk-adjusted performance. EXAM TIP: When comparing investments with different means, ALWAYS use CV, not just standard deviation.

practiceQuestions.question: When calculating SAMPLE variance, we divide by:
practiceQuestions.explanation: Sample variance divides by (n - 1), not n. This is called 'Bessel's correction' and it corrects for the bias that occurs when estimating population variance from a sample. Using (n-1) gives a slightly larger (and more accurate) estimate of variance. EXAM TIP: This is a COMMON exam question. Remember: SAMPLE = (n-1), POPULATION = n.

sections.title: Probability Concepts
sections.content:
WHAT THIS MEANS: Probability is the likelihood that an event will occur. In finance, we use probability to assess risk and expected returns.

BASIC PROBABILITY RULES:

  • Probability ranges from 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%)
  • P(Event) = 0 means the event will NEVER happen
  • P(Event) = 1 means the event will DEFINITELY happen
  • The sum of probabilities of all possible outcomes = 1

EXPECTED VALUE:

The expected value is the probability-weighted AVERAGE of all possible outcomes.

E(X) = Σ[P(x_i) × x_i]

EXAMPLE: A stock has three possible returns:

  • 30% probability of +20% return
  • 50% probability of +10% return
  • 20% probability of -5% return

Expected Return = (0.30 × 20%) + (0.50 × 10%) + (0.20 × -5%) = 6% + 5% - 1% = 10%

JOINT PROBABILITY:

The probability that TWO events BOTH occur.

P(AB) = P(A) × P(B|A)

If events are INDEPENDENT: P(AB) = P(A) × P(B)

EXAMPLE: Probability of Stock A rising = 60%, Probability of Stock B rising = 50%. If independent, probability BOTH rise = 0.60 × 0.50 = 30%.

CONDITIONAL PROBABILITY:

The probability of event A OCCURRING GIVEN THAT event B has already occurred.

P(A|B) = P(AB) / P(B)

EXAMPLE: P(Rain and Traffic) = 20%, P(Rain) = 40%. What's P(Traffic | Rain)?

P(Traffic | Rain) = P(Rain and Traffic) / P(Rain) = 0.20 / 0.40 = 50%

Given that it rains, there's a 50% chance of traffic.

BAYES' FORMULA:

Bayes' formula UPDATES probabilities based on NEW INFORMATION.

P(A|B) = [P(B|A) × P(A)] / P(B)

Where:

  • P(A|B) = Posterior probability (updated probability of A given B)
  • P(B|A) = Likelihood (probability of B given A)
  • P(A) = Prior probability (original probability of A)
  • P(B) = Marginal probability of B

REAL EXAMPLE: A medical test is 95% accurate. Disease occurs in 1% of population. If you test positive, what's the probability you actually have the disease?

Using Bayes' formula:

  • P(Disease) = 0.01 (prior)
  • P(Positive | Disease) = 0.95 (test accuracy)
  • P(Positive) = P(Positive | Disease) × P(Disease) + P(Positive | No Disease) × P(No Disease)
  • P(Positive) = (0.95 × 0.01) + (0.05 × 0.99) = 0.0095 + 0.0495 = 0.059
  • P(Disease | Positive) = (0.95 × 0.01) / 0.059 = 16.1%
Even with a 95% accurate test, you only have a 16.1% chance of having the disease! This is because the disease is so rare (1%).

INDEPENDENT vs. DEPENDENT EVENTS:

  • Independent: One event does NOT affect the probability of the other. EXAMPLE: Flipping a coin twice. The first flip doesn't affect the second flip.
  • Dependent: One event DOES affect the probability of the other. EXAMPLE: Drawing cards from a deck WITHOUT replacement. After drawing one ace, the probability of drawing another ace changes.

MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE EVENTS:

Two events that CANNOT both occur at the same time.

  • EXAMPLE: A coin flip cannot be both heads AND tails
  • For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)

EXAM MEMORY TIP: Remember 'JECE' - Joint, Expected, Conditional, Expected. These are the four probability concepts tested most often.
keyPoints: Probability ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain)
Expected Value = Probability-weighted average of outcomes
Joint Probability = P(AB) = P(A) × P(B) if independent
Conditional Probability = P(A|B) = P(AB) / P(B)
Bayes' Formula updates probabilities with new information
Independent events: one does NOT affect the other
Mutually exclusive events: CANNOT both occur
For mutually exclusive: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)
Sum of all probabilities must equal 1
Memory aid: 'JECE' - Joint, Expected, Conditional, Expected

variables.P(AB): Joint probability of A and B

practiceQuestions.question: A stock has a 40% probability of returning 15%, a 35% probability of returning 8%, and a 25% probability of returning -5%. The expected return is closest to:
practiceQuestions.explanation: E(X) = (0.40 × 15%) + (0.35 × 8%) + (0.25 × -5%) = 6% + 2.8% - 1.25% = 7.55%. EXAM TIP: Expected value is simply the probability-weighted average. Multiply each outcome by its probability, then sum.

practiceQuestions.question: If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, and A and B are independent events, what is P(AB)?
practiceQuestions.explanation: For independent events: P(AB) = P(A) × P(B) = 0.4 × 0.5 = 0.20 or 20%. EXAM TIP: The key word is 'independent.' If events are independent, multiply their probabilities. If they're NOT independent, you need additional information.
